text: The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg to enhance pelvic exposure. The uterus is fixed to the anterior abdominal wall, and the sigmoid colon is secured to the anterolateral abdominal wall using epiploic appendages.

The video snapshot shows the sacral promontory clearly visualized with the peritoneum tented towards it by mesh from the previous hysteropexy. The right ureter is visible lateral to the sacral promontory, positioned medial to the right common iliac artery and its bifurcation. The pouch of Douglas is well exposed without active traction, facilitated by the maximal pelvic exposure achieved through prior positioning steps.

The peritoneum is visibly tented by the mesh extending from the cervix to the sacral promontory, with a previous tack visible through the peritoneum. The surgeon begins dissection by incising the peritoneum to expose the sacral promontory. The mesh from the prior hysteropexy is released to facilitate exposure of the promontory and access the lateral aspect of the rectum.

The surgeon continues the dissection medially to access the rectovaginal space, progressing down to the pelvic floor. The dissection is focused on the rectovaginal space to preserve the autonomic nerves located in the posterior rectal space. A digital examination of the rectum is performed to assist with the dissection down to the rectopelvic junction.

The surgeon places mesh into the peritoneum and introduces PDS suture material through 5mm cannulas. The mesh is secured to the anterior rectal wall with superficial sutures that include the seromuscular layers, ensuring the mucosa remains intact.

The surgeon uses a surgesis biological mesh, known for its resistance to infection and ability to promote tissue remodeling. Four sutures are placed along the lateral aspects of the rectum, with an additional suture placed medially to circumferentially secure the mesh to the rectal wall.

The surgeon pulls the mesh towards the sacral promontory, repositioning the rectum to its original anatomical position. Care is taken to avoid excessive tension that could inadvertently open the anorectal angle, potentially leading to incontinence.

The surgeon palpates the sacral promontory before inserting a tack into the presacral fascia. The tack placement into the peritoneum results in bleeding and demonstrates insufficient tensile strength due to improper fascia incorporation.
The surgeon removes the previously placed tack, palpates the sacral promontory, and inserts a new tack into the presacral fascia overlying the promontory.
The surgeon releases mesh attached to the sacral promontory from a previous hysteropexy to facilitate dissection. The mesh is then sutured together again to preserve the original repair.
The surgeon sutures the intact mesh to the presacral fascia, securing it to maintain the integrity of the original repair at the sacral promontory.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the underlying mesh to prevent adhesion to the small bowel and surrounding structures, completing the ventral rectopexy procedure.
